solution: Both numerator and denominator tend to 0. Since \( \int_{0}^{x} \sin([[a]]t)\,dt = \frac{1 - \cos([[a]]x)}{[[a]]} \) and \( 1 - \cos([[a]]x) \approx \frac{[[a]]^{2}x^{2}}{2} \), the limit is \( [[k]]\cdot \frac{[[a]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies L'Hopital or the Taylor expansion only once, dropping the factor of \( \tfrac{1}{2} \).
This keeps an extra factor of \( [[a]] \), as if the leading term were \( [[a]]^{2}x^{2} \) over \( 2 \) without dividing by \( [[a]] \) once.
This forgets the constant factor \( [[k]] \) out front.
This treats the limit as if the integral contributed only the factor \( [[k]] \).
This uses \( \tfrac{1}{4} \) instead of \( \tfrac{1}{2} \) for the quadratic Taylor coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: This is a single \( 0/0 \) form. By L'Hopital (equivalently, by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the numerator's derivative is \( f(x) \) itself), \( \displaystyle \lim_{x\to 0} \frac{1}{x}\int_{0}^{x} f(t)\,dt = f(0) = [[c]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the limit is automatically 0 without evaluating the indeterminate form.
This reports the linear coefficient of the integrand instead of its value at \( t=0 \).
This evaluates the integrand at \( t=1 \) instead of at \( t=0 \).
This halves the constant term for no valid reason.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: This is again a \( 0/0 \) form at \( x=[[p]] \). By L'Hopital, differentiating the numerator (via FTC-1) gives the integrand itself evaluated at \( x=[[p]] \): the limit equals \( f([[p]]) = ([[a]])([[p]])^{2} + ([[b]])([[p]]) + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the limit is automatically 0 without evaluating the indeterminate form.
This drops the constant term \( ([[c]]) \) from \( f([[p]]) \).
This reports only the constant term, dropping the quadratic and linear contributions at \( t=[[p]] \).
This computes \( f'([[p]]) \) instead of \( f([[p]]) \), differentiating one derivative too many.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: As \( x\to\infty \) both numerator and denominator grow without bound, so L'Hopital applies: differentiating top and bottom, the limit becomes \( \displaystyle \lim_{x\to\infty} \frac{([[m]])x + ([[n]])}{2([[p]])x + ([[q]])} \), which is again an \( \infty/\infty \) form. Differentiating once more (or comparing leading coefficients) gives \( \frac{[[m]]}{2([[p]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This compares the leading linear coefficients directly, forgetting the antiderivative introduces a factor of \( x^2 \) in the numerator (needs the leading coefficient over TWICE the denominator's).
This compares the constant terms of the original integrand and denominator instead of the leading (highest-degree) terms.
This uses the wrong denominator coefficient \( [[q]] \), which does not affect the limit at infinity.
This wrongly assumes a degree-2 over degree-2 ratio must vanish at infinity.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the correct ratio of leading coefficients.

solution: Both accumulation functions vanish at \( x=0 \), giving a \( 0/0 \) form. By L'Hopital, differentiating each (via FTC-1) reduces the ratio to the two integrands evaluated at \( t=0 \): \( \displaystyle \lim_{x\to 0} \frac{([[a]])x + ([[p]])}{([[b]])x + ([[q]])} = \frac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the LINEAR coefficients \( a,b \) of the integrands instead of their values at \( t=0 \).
This inverts the ratio of the two constant terms.
This multiplies the two constant terms instead of dividing them.
This wrongly assumes a ratio of two vanishing quantities must be 0.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct ratio.

solution: The denominator (an accumulation function) vanishes at \( x=0 \) along with the numerator, a \( 0/0 \) form. By L'Hopital, differentiating top and bottom (using FTC-1 on the denominator) gives \( \displaystyle \lim_{x\to 0} \frac{[[m]]}{([[a]])x + ([[p]])} = \frac{[[m]]}{[[p]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( m \) and \( p \) instead of dividing, as if the accumulation function were in the numerator.
This uses the linear coefficient \( a \) of the integrand instead of its constant value \( p \) at \( t=0 \).
This inverts the correct ratio.
This wrongly assumes a ratio of two vanishing quantities must be 0.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct ratio.
